description:
Author Event: Wild Swimming in Aotearoa, Join us for this fabulous event featuring three wild-swimming writers, each inspired in their own unique way by their connection to the ocean. Authors Ingrid Horrocks, Shona Riddell, and Kate Camp will be in conversation with host and wild swimmer extraordinaire Corrina Connor.
Meet our panel:
Ingrid Horrocks is a writer whose work spans poetry, travel, environment, and community. Her many books include the memoir Where We Swim, described as “a book about family that travels by water in the body of a swimmer”, Women Wanderers, as well as two collections of poetry. Her new collection of short stories All Her Lives was published by Te Herenga Waka University Press in October.
Shona Riddell is an author, journalist, podcaster, and swimmer. Her books include Trial of Strength: Adventures and Misadventures on the Wild and Remote Subantarctic Islands and Guiding Lights: The Extraordinary Lives of Lighthouse Women, both published by Exisle Publishing. She has a Master's degree (Science in Society) and hosts a podcast called Swim Chats where she interviews swimmers, coaches, and marine scientists. Shona says: “I like cold weather, being near the ocean (and swimming in it), and learning about remote locations”.
Kate Camp is an award-winning poet and writer. Her first collection of poetry, Unfamiliar Legends of the Stars, won the NZSA Jessie Mackay Award for Best First Book of Poetry at the 1999 Montana New Zealand Book Awards, and she has gone on to publish six more poetry collections, most recently the wonderful Makeshift Seasons (2025). Kate is also a reviewer and essayist and is a familiar voice on Radio NZ.
Meet our host:
Corrina Connor is a marathon swimmer, cellist and one of our very own librarians. Be it the beaches or pools, libraries or music, Corrina makes the most of the opportunities Pōneke provides, in both work and play. In the past few years she has taken on several marathon swims, including Te Moana-o-Raukawa (Cook Strait), Te Ara-a-Kiwa (Foveaux Strait), and Lake Taupō (a mere 40.2 kilometres); as well as undertaking several hypothermia-inducing ‘ice swims’, defined as swimming in water of five degrees or less.
This is a free event -- all welcome. Please note: we expect this event to be extremely popular, and seating will be on a first come first served basis.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Thirty-Six, Jenny and David meet on a tinder date. She wants a one-night stand, and he wants to go through the 36 questions that are meant to lead to love… What unfolds is a night of daring and divulging. Of holding back and holding on. It’s not about happily ever after, it’s about all the people we meet along the way.
Starring Fern Sutherland (Brokenwood Mysteries) Niwa Whatuira (M3gan 2.0) and Anya Tate-Manning (PSA).
Inspired by one of the most esteemed Modern Love articles from The New York Times “The 36 Questions That Lead to Love”. Winner of Best New Play in Bay Area Will Glickman Award 2025.
“A rom-com with an irreverent streak, Thirty-Six is a fun, flirty, occasionally dirty romp. Maybe bring a date. You’ll definitely learn something about each other by the end of the evening” — KQED
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Author Event: Simon Winchester – Breath of the Gods, Join us in the bookshop for our first author event of the year with international superstar writer, SIMON WINCHESTER, in conversation with RNZ's Sally Round.

We are beyond lucky to have Simon include Masterton on this tour, with just two other festival attendances in New Zealand.

The writer behind bestselling non-fiction books, Krakatoa, Land, Exactly and more, Winchester is well known for his ability to take a subject and use it to tell a fascinating a broader story about the world.

In his latest book, The Breath of the Gods, he delivers a thought-provoking history, and future, of that unseen but not unfelt presence in our lives – the wind.

Between the wind as a malevolent force and wind as the saviour of our planet, lies a world of fascination, history, literature, science, poetry and engineering, which Simon Winchester explores with the curiosity and vigour that are the hallmarks of his bestselling works.

Join us for what we’re sure will be a fascinating discussion on how this elemental force shapes the world as we know it.

Tickets are $15 and seating is on first come, first served basis.

Books will be available to purchase and have signed by Simon on the night.

All welcome, see you there!
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
I Don't Know Much About Art But I Know What I Lick, 
I Don't Know Much About Art But I Know What I Lick is a show about fine art for messy people.
The gallery is a space with particular rules of engagement. Young children do not know these rules and embrace art in the way that makes the most sense to them: physically.

In I Don't Know Much About Art But I Know What I Lick, Trick of the Light Theatre (The Bookbinder, The Griegol) join forces with internationally acclaimed performance designer Meg Rollandi on a new show for children (5 and under) and their accompanying adults. Guided by two performer attendants, audiences are invited to explore a gallery set up for kids, featuring satirical and interactive reworkings of iconic art pieces - The Mona Lisa with eyes that actually follow you, the Birth of Venus in a paddling pool clam shell...
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
There Once Was a Boy..., Written and performed by Robert Lloyd

A boy is stranded at sea. A storm bears down on him, and, in what he believes are his final moments, he ponders his life and the choice he made to come here—to explore what’s over the curious horizon—and wonders: Was this a mistake? There once was a boy… is a fun, poetic performance for tweens, teens and their whānau. This live “pop-up book” explores curiosity, courage, and finding light in the darkness.

Recommended for audiences aged 10+. Contains flashing lights.

This show was created as part of the TAHI Festival 2025 Creative Residency programme, in partnership with Creative NZ, PAYPA and Toi Poneke Arts Centre.

description:
Book Launch | Out of the Blue by Christina Barton, Join Te Herenga Waka University Press and Te Pātaka Toi Adam Art Gallery to celebrate the launch of Out of the Blue, an essential collection by Christina Barton that captures four decades of thinking deeply about Aotearoa artists. Thank you to robert heald gallery for hosting the event.
Wednesday 5 November, 5:30 – 7:30pm
robert heald gallery: 230 Leftbank, Cuba Mall, Wellington
All welcome!
In a collection spanning her career, highly regarded art historian and curator Christina Barton reminds readers of the art writer’s essential quandary: how to put the visual, material, sensory and temporal into words. ‘The project of art writing is at once argumentative and invested,’ she writes, ‘self-doubting and ambitious, flawed yet with its own beauty (at its best).’
Published in a partnership between Te Herenga Waka University Press and Te Pātaka Toi Adam Art Gallery, Out of the Blue gathers 37 essays devoted to artists from Aotearoa New Zealand. These are artists whom Barton – entering the art-writing fray in the 1980s, a time of widespread intellectual upheaval – has thought about, worked with and written for, from her first piece on artist and filmmaker Claudia Pond Eyley, published in 1985, to a foreword written in 2021 about sculptor Paul Cullen. They form a small but telling subset of her work, and provide readings that not only anatomise the nature of each artist’s work but also demonstrate the ideas that have been in play as art has unfolded here in Aotearoa.
Artists discussed include Jim Allen, Edith Amituanai, Billy Apple, Bruce Barber, Shane Cotton, Bill Culbert, Pip Culbert, Julian Dashper, Bill Hammond, Louise Henderson, Frances Hodgkins, Zac Langdon-Pole, Maddie Leach, Vivian Lynn, Julia Morison, Kate Newby, Pauline Rhodes, Marie Shannon, Shannon Te Ao and Ans Westra.
The collection is introduced by Elizabeth Eastmond with an afterword by Maddie Leach.
About the author: Christina Barton MNZM is an art historian, curator, art writer, editor and educator. She was director of Te Pātaka Toi Adam Art Gallery from 2007 to 2023 and taught art history at Te Herenga Waka—Victoria University of Wellington from 1995 to 2007. She has curated numerous exhibitions, including the major Billy Apple retrospective at Auckland Art Gallery in 2015. Her writing has been published widely, and she has contributed as an editor of journals Antic and Reading Room, and volumes including the collected art writings of Wystan Curnow. Her major monograph, Billy Apple® Life/Work, was published in 2020.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Sexy Ghost Boy - NZ Fringe 2026, Begin with a circle. Work your way up, slowly. Bravely. The Macarena happens to completion. Scream.

This is how you summon Sexy Ghost Boy, the award winning and hilariously f***able spirit haunting Whiskey & Wood this Fringe.

The show dissects the unspoken rituals and micro neurosis we have around sex and the presentation of ourselves as sexual agents. Satirical in its burlesque and invigorating in its clown, this wordless performance inspires the audience to heroically rebel against insecurity.

Described by witnesses as a hot mess mash up of Mr Bean and Marina Abramović, it has split the sides of audiences across New Zealand and is boldly propositioning Wellingtonians to have an unforgettable evening of Theatre.

Organised Chaos Award / Auckland Fringe 2024
Bizarre and Charming Award / Hasting Fringe 2024
Nominated for Outstanding Performer / Dunedin Fringe 2025
"A theatrical alchemist, Fenn has the rare talent to create something ridiculous yet very clever." - Rosheen Fitzgerald, Theatreview

"I left with a sore throat and vaguely manic euphoria from laughing so uproariously" Ellen Murray, Theatreview

"we both feel so... I don't even know how to describe it. I feel like I'm floating while simultaneously feeling grounded, I'm ecstatic while also very chill. Maybe it's the full moon, or maybe I'm just a little bit possessed by the Sexy Ghost Boy" Laura Ferguson, Art Murmurs
label: Arts & Theatre

RSVP to the survey in the ticketing link to secure your place.

label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Lunchtime author talk | Ingrid Horrocks, Join us at Unity Books Wellington at 12:30 on November 20 to hear Ingrid Horrocks in conversation with her editor, Jasmine Sargent, about her new short story collection All Her Lives.
All Her Lives follows women across generations as they resist, nurture and transform. These are lives shaped by love and politics, motherhood and memory, constraint and defiance.
From girls raised in the garden of Plunket founder Truby King, to a queer university student at a mid-2000s Berlin rave, to a mother facing the cost of her son’s climate rebellion, the women of All Her Lives are complex, resilient and deeply human. Shadowing their stories is the early feminist writer Mary Wollstonecraft, whose journey of grief and revolution will become a vessel for what endures – and for finding hope.
Vast and intimate, All Her Lives explores the layered selfhood of women – all that they inherit, sacrifice, imagine and carry forward – and the power found in unravelling and reweaving those selves on their own terms.
'A wonderful collection that swims in and out of women's lives across time, exploring the struggle for freedom and love. I'll be thinking about it for a long time - a book of quiet force.' —Emily Perkins, author of Lioness
'All Her Lives is a reckoning. A stunning tour de force of women’s lives across centuries, continents and political movements. The collection reads as a protest song book, calling through the ages for change, revolution and peace. Horrocks is one of the most powerful storytellers of our time and All Her Lives is the author's most triumphant work.’ —Laura Jean McKay, author of The Animals in That Country
Ingrid Horrocks’s books include the memoir Where We Swim (2021), a literary history, Women Wanderers and the Writing of Mobility (2017), and two collections of poetry. Her writing has appeared in Lithub, The Ninth Letter, The Sydney Review of Books, The Spinoff, Landfall, and the Guardian. In 2024 she was the Kaituhi Tarāwhare CNZ Writer in Residence at the International Institute of Modern Letters, and in 2025 she was awarded the Michael King Writers Centre Australian Residency at Varuna. Ingrid lives in Te Whanganui-a-Tara, Wellington, with her partner and twin daughters.

description:
Amid the Summer's Malice, 
Amid the Summer's Malice is a re-write of A Midsummer Night's Dream that focuses in on agency and consent, while still keeping the humour. Follow Hermia as she navigates escaping an arranged marriage, gets lost in the streets with her friends Lysander and Demetrius, stumbles into Oberon's love potion plot, deals with the fairy king's meddling, and then come out the other side with Lysander happily in love with her, and she is magically drugged to love him back. Classic Midsummer! Meanwhile, poor Richard (just call him Dick) is putting on a one-man show for the big wedding, and of course, nothing could possibly go wrong there either. With all the magical transformations and love-potions, the implications for agency and consent could not be clearer; so come along to see this funny, colourful story told again, in a modern setting with updated language, and with modern issues at the core.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Club Night - Fireside Chat with Mark and Gavin, it's all about the punt!, Ever wondered what’s really involved in lifting your chains, checking your blocks, and keeping your boat secure inside the harbour?
Come along and hear Gavin and Mark share their knowledge — including Gavin’s tale of installing blocks for Revenue Cutter inside the boat harbour, and how a “mooring muscle boy” became a rocket!
You’ll learn:
How to inspect chains and spot potential issues
Why regular servicing (every 2 years) is the best protection in heavy winds
The story of the Punt — over 100 years old and still servicing moorings in Clyde Quay
The Mooring Punt isn’t a dark art — it’s a simple winch with rollers that does an important job. This is your chance to see how it works, ask questions, and even book a slot to have your mooring serviced so you can sleep well knowing your boat is secure.
label: Hobbies & Interests

description:

No idea is too small, no concept too big, the premise is simple, the answers as complicated as the guests require. A different lineup each night, for a colourful collection of insights.

James Nokise is an internationally award-winning Comedian, Theatremaker, and Podcaster with a history of innovative work in the NZ Fringe Festival, including Rukahu, Faovale Imperium, We Built This City, and Puppet Fiction.

description:
Book Launch | It's What He Would've Wanted by Nick Ascroft, Join us at Unity Books Wellington on 3 September to celebrate a new book from Nick Ascroft! The book will be launched by Always Becominging.
Wednesday 3 September, 6pm
Unity Books Wellington
All welcome!
What would he have wanted? As little fuss as possible. But, reading between the lines: a little help. All the latest gossip and complaints. An arse that is not wrong. Opulence. One leap from the rope ladder. The final word.
It’s What He Would’ve Wanted is the sixth book of poetry from the author of the acclaimed The Stupefying. In this hilarious and affecting new work, Nick Ascroft writes of lost friends, new frailties, new braveries, and being stuck in an organ pipe during a recital and not wanting to bother anyone about it. Yes, there are poems of cycling into dead-end utility holes but also poems of trembling resolve and arriving at work as aged as the night sky after completing the morning school drop-off. This is the best collection yet by one of the most exciting and mercurial poets writing in Aotearoa today.
About the author:
Nick Ascroft is the author of five previous poetry collections with Te Herenga Waka University Press, most recently The Stupefying (2022) and Moral Sloth (2019). Living in Thorndon, he is a public servant and the president of Scrabble Wellington.
Cover design by Todd Atticus.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Photography Symposium – Slow Burn, To celebrate the upcoming photography exhibition Slow Burn | Ahi Tāmau, opening in 2026, we're hosting a day of collegial conversation about contemporary photography in Aotearoa. Slow Burn highlights the contribution of women and non-binary photographers, drawing on over a decade of research and collecting.

Tickets $60 (waged) / $40 (unwaged) with lunch included.

Held on opening day, this symposium begins with a special opportunity to view the exhibition before the museum opens to the public, followed by a keynote presentation from Natalie Robertson, a leading photographer, moving-image artist, and Associate Professor at Auckland University of Technology. Her practice centres her ancestral river, Waiapu, exploring how “lens-based practice can contribute to iwi-based aspirations for environmental reinvigoration”.

Across a series of discussions with Aotearoa photographers, we will explore key threads in contemporary practice – including collaboration, process, and evolving photographic culture – offering insights into the ideas and relationships that shape photography today. We'll close the day in the galleries, with a new performance work by Ann Shelton from the physical garden series.

Open to photographers, curators, researchers, students, and anyone with an interest in photography, this event invites participants to connect, reflect, and share.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Annual Exhibition 2025, Annual Exhibition 2025
Annual Exhibition 2025
The exhibition is being held this year at the Thistle Hall in Cuba St and will run from 29 September to the 5th of October.

Our annual exhibition is the opportunity to showcase the best work of our members to family, friends and the public and we try to include work from each member.

Current members of WPS are invited to submit up to three images to be considered for selection for the exhibition.

Entry
Each member can submit up to three images.
Entries should be sent to by midnight, 30 August 2025.
File Specs: Full resolution under 12MB
File naming: First name, Last Name, Title (if any)
Colour Mode: RGB colour mode (not Grayscale)
Bit Depth: 8 bits/Channel
Colour Space: SRGB
JPEG Format: Baseline (“Standard”)

File Naming
To avoid mixing up each member’s files, each image should have a clear file naming structure. It just allows us to keep better track of the images.
- Number_FirstName_Surname_Title.jpg

Printing and matting
The exhibition will be shown in the club frames, which are 16 x 20 inches. We will arrange printing and mounting of selected images on your behalf. You may elect to print and matt your own images. If this is the case, Please contact us to discuss.

Exhibition dates and times
Thistle Hall, 293 Cuba Street, Te Aro
29 September – 5th October 2025

Opening night
Monday, 29 September | Time 7pm | Thistle Hall, 293 Cuba St, Te Aro. Please come and join us to celebrate the creative photos of your fellow club members and yourself – family and friends are welcome. Finger food and refreshments will be available.

Gallery roster
We are looking to establish a roster of volunteers to man the gallery during weekday afternoons and during the weekends. Please contact the committee via if you can volunteer.
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Not In Our Neighbourhood, Told through the lens of a filmmaker, Not in Our Neighbourhood follows a group of women whose lives intersect at a moment of change. As their stories unfold, what sits beneath the surface begins to show – in quiet ways, in unexpected moments, and in the spaces between what is said and left unsaid.

With warmth, tension, and flashes of humour, the play draws you into a world that feels familiar, yet just out of reach.

An intimate and affecting work that invites you to look closer – and consider what might be happening just beyond view.

Written by Jamie McCaskill
Directors Maaka Pohatu & Paul McLaughlin
Featuring Hariata Moriarty

Sat 23 May – Sat 13 June
Tues – Sat 7.30pm, Sunday 4.30pm
Preview Night Friday 22 May
Sunday Special: Sunday 24 May
Choose Your Price Night: Tuesday 26 May

Duration: 80 minutes
Audience Care: This production contains references to domestic violence, sexual violence, and strong language.
CIRCA TWO
$25 – $60

description:
2026 NZ Card Collectors Show, 2026 NZ Card Collectors Show

It is the most comprehensive card collecting show in the country – over 90 vendors and 200 tables of cards covering all interests and periods (ultra-modern through to vintage).

All cards and collectors catered for, including sports cards (rugby, basketball, cricket, league, football, boxing, ice hockey, UFC etc), TCG (Pokémon, MTG, Yu-gi-oh, Dragonball Z, One Piece, Lorcana etc), trading cards, gum cards, trade cards, cigarette cards, stickers, postcards, as well as associated sports memorabilia, card accessories and card grading services.

Public admission fee:
$5 Adults
Under 18 free

Raffles - Prizes - Games - Live Breaks - Trading Space for Adults and Kids - Food Vendors - Free Parking - Free Valuations

Vendor tables (all 1.8m in length) range in price from $30 for the hobbiest/beginner through to $60 for the business professional. All info for these can be found on the 2026 NZ Card Collectors Show Facebook page.

For more table information/bookings please contact Dan or Steve ()

description:
Haka Mai Senior Kapa Haka Regional Competition, Haka Mai Senior Kapa Haka Regional Competition

Information
All Tickets are Mobile Ticket Only. Mobile Tickets are like Print-at-Home tickets but instead of having to print off the tickets yourself, you can just show the barcode on your mobile phone. It is the easiest way to access tickets to your events. For more information visit Ticketmaster.co.nz/mobileticket Child Policy: Children under 3 years will be admitted free providing they do not occupy a seat (Sit On Lap Policy). Te Whanganui-a-Tara comes alive with haka as 13 kapa take the stage, all vying for one of four coveted spots at Te Matatini 2027. Witness the power, precision, and passion of our region's finest kapa haka as they compete for a place on the national stage. This is more than a competition - it is a celebration of whakapapa, reo, tikanga, and the enduring strength of kapa haka. From spine-tingling moteatea to electrifying haka, this is kapa haka at its highest level - where every movement, every note, and every moment counts.


Note:

description:
Jason Henderson Reads The Telephone Book, 
A high-class dumpster fire re-ingnites.
Celebrate Jason's literacy AND a lifetime's investment in voice and speech training.
Luxuriate in his mellifluous, golden baritone voice as he reprises this 'un-award winning'*, least worthwhile, and most artistically dubious venture of his career.
From 'Aagesen' to 'Zwartz', embark on a declamatory odyssey!
This show premiered as one of many** global events celebrating the 100th anniversary of the 1921 deep sea telephone cable from Key West, Florida, to Havana, Cuba.
**no other events known at time of publication.
Critical Praise:
"Ultimately forgettable."- an actual reviewer (we forget who)
"He studied acting. In New York. For this?" 
"You sounded like you were reading the telephone book." - NYC Acting Coach (If they could see him now... )
"I don't recommend it." - Jason Henderson
"...an insane and inconsequential farrago of sheer fantastic nonsense" - Anna Russell on Mozart's The Magic Flute
*NZ Fringe 2021 Awards - Nominated (Please note: This is NOT an award-winning show - it was *only* nominated)
label: Arts & Theatre

description:
Temporal Aria, Temporal Aria
A breathtaking contemporary circus spectacle exploring the profound tension between control and surrender.

About this Event


WINNER - Momentous Movement Award (NZ Fringe 2025)
WINNER - Take Your Breath Away Award (Reykjavik Fringe 2025)

Step into a world where gravity bends and the boundaries of the human body are redefined. Temporal Aria is a breathtaking contemporary circus experience that fuses the ancient art of hair suspension with acrobatics to explore the profound tension between control and surrender.
Inspired by performer Nicole Maisey’s recent ADHD diagnosis, the show becomes a deeply personal exploration of distractibility, executive dysfunction, and the pursuit of inner peace. Through feats of extraordinary strength and vulnerability, Nicole transforms struggle into artistry, expressing a journey toward balance and self-acceptance.
At its heart, Temporal Aria is about learning to release control and accept support, a powerful shift from isolation to connection. As Nicole navigates the physical and emotional extremes of her art, she discovers that surrender is not weakness, but a pathway to strength, resilience, and unexpected grace.
Set to a mesmerizing soundscape, Temporal Aria is both a spectacle and a meditation. a poetic intersection of circus, emotion, and identity. It’s not just about the act of letting go, but about what we discover in the process: joy, contentment, and the redefinition of success as peace within.
Prepare to be captivated by a performance that truly takes your breath away.

description:
Messy NZ Fringe 2026, Gracie & Freya’s lives are a mess.

They’ve graduated art school but they still need to make it.

Until then, they are still crammed together in their one bedroom flat with Freya sleeping in the living room.

Gracie has caved and gone back to retail work at the house of G. Which leaves no time for art, even if she could bring herself to think about it.
Freya is home all day making mushrooms . Clay mushrooms that are starting to pile up around the house and send Gracie mad.
Also, Freya might- accidentally -sort of -kind of -have become drug dealer .

Everything is copasetic, except that Gracie can’t stop having panic attacks at work and plotting people’s deaths- especially her therapist’s. She’s so filled with anger she can’t see straight.
She certainly can’t see Freya who might need more support than she lets on.

Messy is a dark comedy from writer Tara Weston-Webb. Fresh off a development run in 2025. The work has been re-configured for the random stage and a Fringe audience.

“The intelligent, quipping dialogue is somewhere between Gilmore Girls and an Aaron Sorkin series. Most twenty to thirty somethings will recognise themselves or their friends in these characters, their relationship and experiences. The audience is a third, silent confidant in their rhythmic relationship.” (Niven, The Wellingtonista)

The subtle underpinning of the play looks at our art industry; how tough it can be not to give up on this career & how necessary mental health support is for our resilience.

The writing is snappy but humanising. It pokes fun at the mundane drudgery of life and highlights the beauty of creating art and leaning on your artistic community for support.

The play came about from real conversations between Tara and her peers- actors, clothing designers, visual artists,, musicians, photographers, directors, singers, dancers and chefs who all felt that education in their field, or time in the field itself kicked the stuffing out of them, and left them utterly drained on the doorstep of real life. But who also were not willing to give up on their dreams.

It’s also for anyone who’s battled with their mental health and through the hurdles of asking for and accepting help.

Viewers of Messy are in for an evening of physical and intellectual comedy. As well as an introspective look at the different ways mental health struggles present. With detailed set design and gorgeous lighting design from Em Maguire.

Come ready to laugh and stay for the emotional heart of the relationship between two devoted friends.

Messy pokes fun at the mundane drudgery of life, and highlights the beauty of creating art and leaning on your artistic community for support.

description:
Ben Miller: Volcano, 
What happens when a comedian walks into a volcano?
In 2023, Ben Miller was the artist in residence at Hawai'i Volcanoes National Park, the first ever stand-up comedian ever selected. This was probably a terrible idea on their part.
Come watch this show where scientist turned stand-up comedian Ben Miller explores volcanology, entomology, Hawaiian history, space volcanoes, cats, cookies, and more.
Ben Miller is an NYC based scientist turned comedian who has been working for the past 9 years. In that time, he has performed at all the top clubs in the city such as Broadway Comedy Club, Carolines, and The Stand. Ben was once ranked as the top roast battler in NYC. He has a degree in Materials Science and Engineering, worked on a science bus, 3D printed cookies and has defused a few bar fights while on stage. In 2022, Ben Miller's debut hour, Stand-Up Science, had a completely sold-out run at the Edinburgh Fringe Festival and he now tours internationally.
 
Praise for the show:
 
"Ben Miller's nerdy and vivid comic mind erupts in this hour-long set!" - Winnipeg Free Press (4-star review)
"Ben does a fantastic job of getting the facts across and making us laugh at the same time! A real and rare treat. - The Mumble (4-star review)
"Ben had us laughing until we cried - very funny and very clever! - Audience member

description:
FEBRUARY: The Idiot by Fyodor Dostoevsky, We return to Russia for our long summer read! The group's first ever summer book was Anna Karenina and we read Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov over the 2018/19 summer. (We were also due to discuss his novel Crime and Punishment, but that was set down for April 2020 and, well, the world had other plans…)
This year we read another of Fyodor Dostoevsky's masterworks: The Idiot, which was initially published as a serial in 1868-1869. Dostoevsky scholar Joseph Frank said The Idiot "is the most personal of all his major works, the book in which he embodies his most intimate, cherished, and sacred convictions".
The story revolves around Prince Lef Nicolaievitch Muishkin, a young man returning to Russia after spending several years in a Swiss sanatorium for epilepsy. Dostoevsky had set out to create a protagonist with "a truly beautiful soul" and to trace the fate of such an individual as he comes into contact with the brutal reality of contemporary society.
Photo: The Body of the Dead Christ in the Tomb by Hans Holbein, which features in the novel.
VENUE
We meet in the ‘Games Room’, The Library's private area overlooking Courtenay Place. After you enter the bar, head to your right (up the step) and through a smaller space with booths to the large carpeted space beyond.
SPREADSHEET OF BOOKS READ

The day is more than a game, it's a time to come together, celebrate James’s life, and remind ourselves of the strength and unity in our community. It also highlights the importance of looking out for one another and supporting mental health awareness.

description:
Skate or Die, 
A crew of die-hard skater boys get sucked into the video game Skate or Die. At first, it's all sick tricks, banter, and bros, until the game starts to glitch. Hard. To escape, they'll have to do more than land a clean ollie. This game is wired to force a reckoning with their past, their egos, their fragility and fears. 
Created by Ōtautahi artist Millie Hanford and performed by five powerhouse women, Skate or Die fuses satire, movement, and gaming culture in a bold theatrical collision of comedy, chaos and actual skateboarding. Expect laughter, discomfort, and the kind of truth that hits like a fall from a halfpipe.
"I felt so seen, like I knew each of those guys."
"I can't believe you had Grizzly grip tape on your board!" 
From Pretty Little Sweethearts, this is the feminist revenge fantasy of your dreams, disguised as a video game. Funny, furious, and unflinchingly relevant.

description:
Four Questions, 
Four-year-olds ask the biggest questions. As we grow, the answers get tangled--gender, identity, belonging, the slow loss of magic. In this contemporary dance work, a community cast explores moments of wonder, confusion and discovery--reminding us how it feels to see the world again with fresh eyes. Four Questions moves through the stretch and the ache of becoming, and finds, somewhere in the mess, a spark still glowing. Is this proof that the magic never really left?

description:
Embarrassed Naked Female (this show contains nudity) at NZ Fringe, A lecture-performance following a stolen video down the rabbit hole of internet creeps -- the true story of five nude minutes sparking years of obsessive sharing, bizarre commentary, and surreal online encounters. A darkly humorous dive into consent in the digital age.

In 2020, during the first Covid lockdown, I made my solo show "The Sensemaker" temporarily available online.
Strangely, the video quickly became highly sought-after in certain corners of the internet -- all because of the five minutes of nudity it contains. Years later, I'm still having to take it down from various adult forums where it keeps resurfacing without my consent.
In trying to track where it had been shared, I stumbled into a bizarre world of posts and comments.
Let me read you a few.

"Embarrassed Naked Female (this show contains nudity)" has won the Virtuoso Award (Stockholm Fringe Festival 2025) and the Hot Potato Award (Gothenburg Fringe 2025).

This show is presented in tandem with "The Sensemaker".

description:
RNZ Podcast launch – Pūtātara: Revolutions in Māori Art, Join us to celebrate the upcoming launch of new limited series podcast, Pūtātara: Revolutions in Māori Art.
Hosted by Matariki Williams and produced by Jamie Tahana, Pūtātara dives into the sociopolitical context around which Māori art has evolved and revolved, opening with the first Ngā Puna Waihanga hui in Te Kaha in 1973, and taking us right up to 2025.
We journey with artists to the landmark show Te Maori opening at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in 1984, the opening of Te Papa in 1998, along with key events that have offered new ways of thinking about Māori art.
Critically, the podcast examines the role of the museum and the gallery – then and now – and explores what Māori artists aspire to in 2026 and beyond.
Pūtātara joins Tukua and Orators Anonymous as part of a trio of arts and culture podcasts commissioned through a Creative New Zealand, NZ On Air, and RNZ co-fund, in response to the New Mirrors research, which focused on how we might strengthen arts and culture media in Aotearoa New Zealand.
Doors open at 5:30pm, for a 6:00pm start. A complimentary drink will be provided per person. Please RSVP via the Eventbrite page.

description:
Historic Anzac Hall Tours, Enjoy a fascinating guided tour of the historically significant Anzac Hall.
The hall was first opened in 1916 as a recreation centre for the troops at the nearby Featherston military camp, with the families of the first European settlers donating the funds. The building was used as a hospital during the influenza epidemic of 1918-19. It is a Category One Historic Place.
The walk around of our fabulous Anzac Hall will provide visitors of the history of the hall, its usage over the years and its importance to Featherston today.
There are four tours:
Saturday 8th Nov: 1.30 - 2.15pm, 2.30 - 3.15pm
Sunday 9th Nov: 1.30 - 2.15pm, 2.30 - 3.15pm
Koha entry
This event is part of the Wellington Heritage Festival

⏰ Time: 3:30 PM – 4:30 PM (arrive at 3.15pm)
🎟️ Tickets: FREE – but limited to 20 NAWIC members only
🛠️ What You’ll See and Hear About:
Importance Level 4 Building
Challenges of designing and building in an airport environment
Sustainable features of the build
Landscape improvements and viewing areas
💬 Why You Should Come
👷‍♀️ Learn from an active, high-profile infrastructure project
🧯 Gain insights into the unique requirements of an airport fire station build
🤝 Network with fellow NAWIC members and industry professionals
🛠️ See how construction is adapting to new aviation technologies
🦺 PPE requirements (hard hats, hi-vis, glasses, steel-capped boots). We have spare hi-vis, hard-hats and safety glasses available – please advise us in your RSVP if you require any help with this.
This is a NAWIC members-only event with limited capacity — please book your space consciously! 
🙏 A special thank you to Wellington Airport and Naylor Love for generously hosting this site visit and supporting women in construction.
We look forward to seeing you there! 🚒
Please note: NAWIC is a voluntary organisation, committed to its obligations as a PCBU under the Health and Safety at Work Act 2015.   Amongst other things, this requires NAWIC to take all practicable steps to eliminate, isolate or minimize hazards that our event attendees are exposed to. Health and safety is a shared responsibility.

description:
River City Express, River City Express
Travel to the beautiful river side town of Whanganui, hauled by both steam locomotive Ab 608 and diesel locomotive Da 1410. Choose between doing your own thing, or jumping on a serene cruise of the Whanganui River onboard the coal fired paddle steamer "PS Waimarie".

Leaving Kapiti as most others are still sound asleep, we quickly escape the coast and delve into the rurality of the Horowhenua. After a stop in Palmerston North we head up the trunk through the townships of Feilding and Marton before turning onto the Marton - New Plymouth railway line for the last stretch into Whanganui. After crossing the 290km long Whanganui River, we proceed slowly down the short 5km branch line taking us to Taupo Quay.

TRAVEL ON THE PADDLE STEAMER WAIMARIE!
Salvaged from the bottom of the Whanganui River where she sat for around 50 years, the Paddle Steamer Waimarie was restored to her former glory and re-launched in 2000. She is now the only one of her kind in operation in New Zealand. Your cruise leaves the Riverboat Centre Wharf and heads upriver taking in the scenery and views of Whanganui which can only be experienced from Te Awa Tupua, the Whanganui River. The commentary shares riverboat history and points of interest along the way. Check out the boiler that powers our paddle steamer, and maybe meet the homing pigeons and send a message with them on their flight back to the Riverboat Centre.

Departing Paekakariki/Paraparaumu/Waikanae/Otaki/Levin
Adult 13+ yrs - $209
Child 2-12yrs - $179

Departing Palmerston North/Feilding
Adult 13+ yrs - $189
Child 2-12yrs - $169

Buses will transport you from your arrival at the station to the Waimarie River Boat Centre.

DO YOUR OWN THING!
There will be a collection of vintage cruiser bikes for hire for a small fee to those keen to take exploring to the next level.

There will be short tours available at the Durie Hill Elevator. It is New Zealand’s only public transport elevator and is still used on a daily basis by locals and visitors.

There are multiple places to go, and things to see on a cruiser bike or on foot. Take a tram ride for a small fee and arrive at the Riverboat museum, be blown away by NZ Glassworks, admire the sculptures and views from the Motua gardens along the river, indulge in local cuisine, or simply shop local.

Departing Paekakariki/Paraparaumu/Waikanae/Otaki/Levin
Adult 13+ yrs - $159
Child 2-12yrs - $139

Departing Palmerston North/Feilding
Adult 13+ yrs - $139
Child 2-12yrs - $119

Book at https://steaminc.org.nz/book-train-trip/river-city-express/

description:
October Full Moon Drumming, Please remember this is a ZERO WASTE and *NO ALCOHOL* community gathering.
Kia ora!
Full Moon Drumming Wellington is a registered public event with the Wellington City Council. We are a group of humans from all walks of life who gather under the full moon each month to connect through drumming and dance around the fire. This gathering is participatory, that means if you made it to the circle you are part of the circle. As in Africa, there is no audience! Everyone is invited to participate as part of the ‘chorus’ (singing/clapping), dancing, or drumming.
The event is weather permitting, we check the forecast a few days prior and aim to make a decision the day before the event, although this is not possible and sometimes we make the decision on the day. All updates are posted on the event page.
This gathering is entirely run by volunteers, if you'd like to contribute in any way please get in touch.
There are a few things that we need help with:
- FIREWOOD: Please bring dry firewood as a koha, no wood no fire! Please NOTE we DO NOT burn pallets (due to nails) or treated timber. Driftwood from nearby south coast beaches is often abundant, feel free to post or reach out if you have a vehicle and are keen to help gather firewood on the day of the event.
- BEAUTIFYING THE CIRCLE SPACE: Anyone has fairy lights, oil burners, or is keen to make some signs?
NOTE: We hold a fire permit for this event. For safety reasons we don't allow participants to feed the fire, and while is generally okay to roast some marshmallows early in the evening we don't allow the use the fire for cooking or heating food.
This is an **ALCOHOL FREE EVENT**, please help us uphold this kaupapa and remind those who are unaware and might be new to the event. As hosts we are responsible for your safety and we take that responsibility seriously, especially given that the event is authorised through both fire and event permits. Large, public, all ages gatherings require administration and logistics so please respect this and the guidelines provided.
Princess Bay is within Taputeranga Marine Reserve (https://octopus.org.nz/taputeranga-marine-reserve), we are welcomed to gather each month in this special place so let’s all make sure there is NO RUBBISH LEFT BEHIND and that we take care of the space. Dogs are NOT ALLOWED at the gathering - there are Korora (Blue Pinguins) nesting under the rocks nearby and we've had issues with dogs in the past.
Children are welcome at the fire, though, especially after dark, please make sure that all children have adult supervision, that the supervisors are aware of the children they are looking after, and that the children are aware of who is looking after them.
Feel free to bring:
- Your friends.
- Drums or any percussive instrument.
- Warm clothing; jackets and wool blankets are good.
- Something to seat on (otherwise sand is often wet)
- Drinking water.
- Food, tea to share.
- Your lovely selves.
- Fire spinning kit and fuel.
FIRE SPINNERS: Please take all precautionary safety measures and make sure to set up fire-spinning downwind from the fire circle.
PARKING: There's a parking lot a few hundred meters from the fire circle where there's also public toilets. Please also be aware of our neighbors driveway opposite the stairway and do not park directly in front of it.
CARPOOLING: If you don't have a way to get to Princess Bay, or if you have spare seats in your car, feel free to post it on the event page. The less cars driving the better.
Feel free to share the event page and send an event invite to whomever you feel would be interested, not everyone sees the notifications and advertising

description:
A Change Is Coming Walk Tour, 2026 marks the 40th anniversary of the passing 1986 of the Homosexual Law Reform Act. The legislation set out to decriminalise homosexual activity between consenting adults and make it illegal to discriminate against someone based on their sexual orientation.

But this wasn’t the first attempt at homosexual law reform. Join us on this special 90-minute free walk tour to celebrate the people and movements that fought for a brighter future for rainbow communities.

Our journey begins at the location of the first public meeting in New Zealand, advocating for homosexual law reform in 1967. This was two years before the Stonewall uprising in the U.S., which went on to ignite Gay Liberation around the world.

The tour ends at Parliament, where we honour the Members of Parliament who have championed equality over the last five decades.

Along the way, we’ll reflect on some of the difficult realities rainbow communities have faced, from the injustices of the legal system, to the stances of the medical profession, churches and the military. As the late activist Ralph Knowles recalls, “As far as medicine was concerned, it was sick. As far as the law was concerned, it was a crime. As far as the church was concerned, it was a sin.”

Though at times the stories are challenging, this walk tour is ultimately uplifting, as we pay tribute to our amazing rainbow kaumatua and allies who led the way.

description:
Greytown Heritage Trust House and Garden Tour 2026, This is another opportunity to explore charming Greytown houses and gardens where the owners have valued and enhanced the integrity of the building and its environment, and so nurtured Greytown's heritage.

Greytown Heritage Trust offers this self-guided tour, unveiling nine remarkable properties along with three destination events. A newly opened countryside café, spoiling with on-site baking, offers a pause looking out towards the Tararua range. A barbecue in a bucolic Waiohine setting. A fête, nostalgically redolent of yesteryear, selling perennials, crafts and home produce. There will be Devonshire cream teas and Wairarapa Rosé and bubbly, bunting, adventure and indulgence. How could you resist?
The houses are further flung than the previous tour and we listened hard to feedback – fewer destinations but greater distances will mean you’ll more comfortably explore this charming town and it’s houses and gardens.
You’re invited to spend a summer’s day exploring remarkable houses and gardens in this attractive town. In so doing you will contribute to a fundraiser for the refurbishment of Greytown's colonial-settler Kōuka Cottage (c1860) at Stella Bull Park, 113 Main Street, Greytown. This cottage and its maintenance are important in fostering Greytown's architectural heritage.
Receive with your ticket:
Access to 9 of Greytown’s significant private homes & gardens. Access to a fête, a café and a countryside barbecue.
A quality keepsake folding map & information poster. These will be posted to you nearer to the date.

General information & conditions
Properties are open from 10.00am until 4.30pm. This tour is self-guided; you can visit the properties in any order you wish.
Each ticket holder will be sent a map and wristbands for entry, by post. It is absolutely critical that you include your address on your Eventfinda registration and purchase of tickets.
At each venue you’re asked to present your wristband to the volunteers at the entrance to gain access. Please ensure that you are wearing your wristband during the tour as this will ensure your entry at each address. The wristband isn't transferable to other people during the tour events.
The houses, of course, are private homes. Photographs are available on the Greytown Heritage Trust’s media sites and we ask that you don’t take your own photographs inside houses, nor touch furnishings or items. Some areas will be inaccessible and signed accordingly. Some gardens may also be off limits for photography but this will be indicated at the venue.
Keeping people and property safe is important, this tour is reserved for those aged over 15. We’re also asking that no animals are brought to the visits unless they are mobility-trained and certified. Accessibility is compromised variously in houses and some gardens.
Greytown public toilets will be marked on the map.
The tour will proceed regardless of rain or other moderately poor weather but there may be a cancellation in the event of Met Office amber or red warnings. In this, what we hope is unlikely, event we will communicate with you by email and on social media sites.
Cash sales and some eftpos facilities will be available at some properties. The proceeds or proportions of these sales will also contribute to the fundraiser.
The Trust is grateful for your support, and wish you a wonderful day exploring and enjoying Greytown’s heritage properties.
Links
Website (for more heritage info):
https://www.greytownheritagetrust.co.nz/
Social Media (for sneak peaks of the tour):
https://www.instagram.com/greytownheritagetrust/
https://www.facebook.com/greytownheritagetrust

description:
Heritage Festival Open Days, Heritage Festival Open Days
Join us for an open day in the bell tower of Wellington Cathedral of St Paul, where you can uncover its unique history and see the bellringers come out to play.

Take a tour through our ringing chamber and belfry, and see our 140-year-old bells up close. You will have a chance to watch the ringers in action, and maybe even have a go at chiming our 1500kg tenor bell, the largest swinging bell in New Zealand! Discover the secret world of bells and bellringing, something the public doesn't normally get to see. You may have never known that people are pulling on the ropes to make the bells ring! *One of the few towers in the world accessible by lift!

**Entry by donation**

Health & Safety
Please be aware that touching the bell ropes and access to the bells outside of the designated viewing areas is strictly prohibited. You will only be permitted to handle a bell rope under the guidance and supervision of a bellringer. Hearing protection is not required in the ringing chamber, but earmuffs will be provided whilst in the belfry viewing gallery if the bells are going to be rung.

Location
James Thomas Bell Tower, Wellington Cathedral of St Paul, 2 Hill Street, Thorndon 6011

Go By Bike Day - Queens Wharf, Ride on down to Queens Wharf and join Wellington City Council for Go By Bike Day - a celebration of getting around by pedal power in the city!

When: Tuesday 10 February 2026, 7.30am - 9.00am
Where: Under the sails, Queens Wharf, Wellington

Bike or no bike, everyone's invited! Come enjoy some free kai, coffee and good chats to start your day right.

Go By Bike Day celebrates cycling in Wellington Pōneke as part of the Aotearoa Bike Challenge - a national campaign encouraging more people to get cycling for February (and beyond!). If you haven’t yet signed up for the Challenge, sign up on the day and go in the draw to win a Specialised E-Bike worth $3500!

A huge thanks to local partners and volunteers, including Dough Bakery, Cycle Wellington, EkeRua ReBicycle, Switched on Bikes, Flamingo, Greater Wellington Regional Council, and many others for making this event possible. This event will also be supported by Mug. Cycle (mugcycle.nz) who will provide mugs for hot drinks - though please bring a reusable cup if you can.

Let's get rolling together Pōneke!

description:
Wairarapa Walking Festival 2025: Ruamāhanga Awa Sundowner Walk, Back by popular demand after delighting festival-goers in 2024, this unique twilight walk returns for 2025. Led by Jane & Rod from the Ruamāhanga Farm Foundation, the journey begins with a sensory—and at times silent—stroll along the Ruamāhanga River before entering the majestic Waihinga Bush, one of the finest examples of podocarp forest near Martinborough.

As the sun sets and the forest darkens, take your place beneath the towering trees and be transported by a mythical tale of how this special forest came to be. A truly atmospheric experience, blending nature, story, and stillness.

Registration cost will be donated to Ruamāhanga Farm Foundation.

Walk Format:
This 4km walk is predominantly on flat track, with mown grass underfoot, however the ground can be uneven.

description:
Wellington Vintage Machinery Open Days, Wellington Vintage Machinery Open Days
Get a close look and hands on experience with machinery of yesteryear. Weather permitting, we will have our Fowler Traction engine in steam too!

Find the machine that was used to make the 1950 Empire Games medals. Many of the machines have a association with the Wellington area.

Take a nostalgic trip through the vintage kitchen and other displays of household, and retail items from the past. Wellington Vintage Machinery is housed in an old Cheese Factory built in 1925. The factory has had an interesting past from cheese factory, to milk bottling plant, munitions store during WWII, and other uses before becoming home for the vintage machinery.

The building is one of the few remaining dairy factories designed by architects Duffill & Gibson of Hawera and built by CE Roebuck of New Plymouth.


Cost: Koha
Event information here: https://wellingtonheritagefestival.co.nz/event/wellington-vintage-machinery-open-days-25/


Health & Safety:
Please wear closed toe footwear and keep an eye on any children that you bring along.

Most of the display is wheelchair accessible, and an accessible toilet facility is also available.

description:
The Founding Families of St. Joseph's Pāuatahanui, The Founding Families of St. Joseph's Pāuatahanui
Hear the stories, with accompanying photos and maps, which bring to life the families who founded this beautiful church 147 years ago.

Brian Murphy, a descendant of the Murphy family who built St. Joseph's church, will lead this talk. A small community of closely-linked families, with many coming from Roscommon in Ireland, established farms at Pāuatahanui and donated funds to build this timber Victorian-Gothic church.

Brian will relate stories which lie behind the graves and unique windows of St. Joseph's.


Cost: Koha
Event information here: https://wellingtonheritagefestival.co.nz/event/the-founding-families-of-st-josephs-pauatahanui/


Health & Safety:
The site is a little steep. Limited site parking is available for easier access. Good shoes and an umbrella are advised.

description:
Communicate Across Generations: Bridge the Divide, Tired of miscommunications with your Gen Z niece or your Baby Boomer parent? This one-day workshop will equip you with the tools to navigate the diverse communication styles of today's multi-generational world. (NZ citizen and residents free with ID, all others pay listed price).

This course is free for NZ citizens and residents. Please enrol online and bring photo ID when attending the workshop.

If you are not a NZ citizen or resident, a fee of $120 applies and you must phone the office on 04 385 8919 to register.

Discover why different generations prioritise different things in their interactions, from family group chats and face-to-face conversations through to workplace attitudes. Learn to decode the nuances, bridge the divides, and foster more effective communication in your daily life.

Learning Outcomes

Deconstruct Communication Styles: You will be able to identify and describe the typical communication styles, preferences, and values of different generations, including Baby Boomers, Generation X, Millennials, and Generation Z.

Mitigate Conflict: You will learn to recognise and resolve common intergenerational communication conflicts by understanding their root causes and applying effective conflict resolution strategies.

Improve Collaboration: You will develop practical skills to adapt your communication approach to better engage with people from different generations, leading to enhanced understanding and connection.

Foster Inclusivity: You will be able to appreciate and leverage the unique perspectives and strengths that each generation brings to the table, creating more inclusive environments in your relationships.

Tea and coffee provided, please bring your lunch and snacks, and pen and paper to take notes.

Note: Courses need a minimum number to go ahead so if we don't have enough enrolments a few days before the start date, we have to cancel. We'll let you know by email so please check yours regularly. If you need to cancel your registration, here are our terms and conditions: "What You Need to Know".

description:
Voice of Aroha Anniversary Gala 2025, ✨ Experience a Night of Celebration and Impact in Wellington! ✨
Join us at the Voice of Aroha Anniversary Gala on Saturday, November 1st, 2025, for an unforgettable evening of reflection, connection, and aroha. 🎉 Immerse yourself in inspiring stories, captivating performances, and the warmth of community — all while making a real difference in the lives of refugee and migrant communities across Aotearoa.
🌟 Voice of Aroha 6th Anniversary Gala 🌟
For six incredible years, Voice of Aroha has amplified untold voices, empowered youth, and brought people together through storytelling, leadership, and celebration.
💡 This year has been filled with achievements and new opportunities for our young people, but it has also been one of the toughest yet, with limited funding. To keep our mahi alive and ensure our youth continue to thrive, we need your support. Will you stand with us?
🗓️ Date: Saturday, November 1, 2025
🕕 Time: 6:00 PM – 9:00 PM
📍 Location: The Hub, Kelburn Campus (Te Herenga Waka – Victoria University of Wellington)
✨ What to Expect:
🌟 Inspiring youth voices
🌟 Beautiful cultural performances
🌟 Dinner with whānau for a great cause
🌟 Raffle tickets + prizes to be won 🎟️
🌟 A night of aroha, unity & impact
💫 Every ticket is a donation. Your generosity directly supports Voice of Aroha’s ongoing mahi — empowering youth, creating inclusive spaces, and ensuring refugee and migrant voices are heard, valued, and celebrated.
🎟️ Get your ticket today and be part of this milestone celebration! Together, we’ll ensure our mission not only continues but grows stronger, making a lasting difference for generations to come.

description:
Mirimiri Community Clinic Day GLENSIDE, Mirimiri and Romiromi is traditional Māori bodywork and healing. Treatment is holistic and includes body alignment, deep tissue work and release of stress and tension by applying pressure to specific points on the body.
This will be our second Community Clinic Day at our Glenside clinic.
If you have never experienced Mirimiri or Romiromi before, this is your opportunity! Treatment is beneficial for conditions of pain, misalignment, sports or work injuries and relief from emotional distress, stress & tension; while also supporting the body to realign and rebalance energetically.
Sessions: 20mins. COST: $20
Please ensure you are well with no cold or flu symptoms on the day.
One booking per person please (no double appointments)
Payments are non-refundable but may be transfered to another person on the day.
If you are on ACC or require a full consult please contact us directly to book in.
Thankyou for your co-operation and we look forward to seeing you there!

description:
Rainbow Elders: Our Aspirations, Our Well-Being, Our Rights - a panel discussion, Rainbow Elders: Our Aspirations, Our Well-Being, Our Rights - a panel discussion
Te Kāhui Tika Tangata Human Rights Commission is proud to host this discussion for International Day of Older Persons in partnership with Kawe Mahara Queer Archives Aotearoa (formerly LAGANZ).

Panelists from the Wellington rainbow community - 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗶 𝗠𝗶𝘁𝗰𝗵𝗲𝗹𝗹, 𝗞𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗻 𝗛𝗮𝘂𝗻𝘂𝗶 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗟𝗮𝗶𝗻𝗲𝘆 𝗖𝗼𝘄𝗮𝗻 - will join Chief Commissioner Dr Stephen Rainbow for the discussion and a brief Q and A session. 🌈🎙️💫

Prudence Walker, Disability Rights Commissioner and Rainbow rights spokesperson will host the panel.

𝗣𝗿𝗲-𝗿𝗲𝗴𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝘀 𝗿𝗲𝗾𝘂𝗶𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗳𝗼𝗿 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝘀 𝗳𝗿𝗲𝗲 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗱𝘂𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗹𝗶𝗺𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗱 𝘀𝗲𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴. Pre-register here: https://events.humanitix.com/rainbow-elders-our-aspirations-our-well-being-our-rights-a-panel-discussion 🎟️🎟️🎟️

The discussion runs from 10.30am to 11.30am.

NZSL will be available. The venue is accessible.

Light refreshments will be available.

A recording of the discussion may be made available afterwards via the Commission's website.
❤️🧡💛💚💙💜🩷

description:
Wave of Light 2025, Hōkai Tahi invites you to attend our Wave of Light memorial to remember all our babies gone too soon.
We'll gather at the Carrington Park Rotunda from 6.45pm for a 7pm candle lighting and minute of silence.
Bring along a candle to add to our memorial display - lanterns or jars are a good idea if it is breezy.
We'd love to see photos of your babies too.
Stay for a hot drink (provided) and add your babies name to our memory tree.
All welcome!
This event is held at the end of Baby Loss Awareness Week 9th - 15th October.
About the International Wave of Light:
The International Wave of Light invites baby loss families, friends, loved ones and supporting organisations from around the world to join in honour and remembrance of all loved and longed for babies gone too soon.
No matter where you are in the world, you can light a candle at 7pm in memory of your baby or babies to be part of the wave of light that begins here in Aotearoa and wraps around the globe.
Wherever you do this, you will be joining a global ‘Wave of Light’ in memory of all the babies who lit up our lives for such a short time.
This is a family friendly event, please be mindful pregnant families, babies, children, as well as health professionals wishing to support our community may be in attendance
label: Community & Culture

description:
Sam Bambery - Live at Vogelmorn Upstairs, Sam Bambery - Live at Vogelmorn Upstairs
Sam Bambery is an artist and impresario from Ōtautahi/Christchurch, currently based in Naarm/Melbourne. Exploring his love of semi-psychedelia through a twisted folk-rock lens, Bambery writes effectual and spacious songs burrowing into emotional identity and exhaustion. His sophomore album Rubricator released in 2024 with micro-label Under Underground Records, edging at outsider-pop influence.


"...very considered, like Bambery lived with the album in his head for a while before bringing it into the world...a hint of mystery hangs over things...it's as ornate as these well-crafted tunes" - Tony Stamp, Radio New Zealand

"Rubricator confirms the emergence of a real talent" - Graham Reid, NZ Listener

Sam Bambery is joined by Michael Llewellyn
label: Music & Concerts

description:
Fiesta! Fiesta! tickets, Presented by: The Aotearoa Jazz Orchestra
The Aotearoa Jazz Orchestra presents: Fiesta! Fiesta! 🔥
This winter, the heat arrives early.
Join the Aotearoa Jazz Orchestra for a spectacular night of rhythm, colour, and irresistible Latin groove as the country’s national jazz orchestra joins forces with an all-star lineup of Aotearoa’s finest musicians from the Latin music community.
From the pulse of Afro-Cuban rhythms to the fire of salsa and the lush sounds of Latin jazz, Fiesta! Fiesta! is a celebration of the rich musical traditions of Latin America — brought vividly to life by the extraordinary talent living here in Aotearoa.
Expect soaring brass, driving percussion, and music that will lift you out of the depths of winter and onto your feet.
Warm the heart. Move the body. Celebrate the music.

description:
Mason Brothers, Wayne and Paul, Wayne Mason and Paul Bowers-Mason team up to perform this acoustic, story-driven gig featuring hits from Wayne's career, including 'Nature'.

After their previous sold-out performances last year, Mason Brothers are taking their show on the road and will join us in Wellington.

Wayne Mason is a Kiwi music icon best known for writing NZ's best song "Nature" for the Fourmyula, later covered by the Muttonbirds. He was also a founding member of the Warratahs, playing piano and writing their first hit song "Hands of My Heart".

In this show Wayne and younger brother Paul Bowers-Mason will play all the best songs from Wayne's long career in NZ music. It's an acoustic sound with Wayne on vocals and either guitar or piano, while Paul does backing vocals and plays upright bass. Expect some interesting stories too about how the songs were written and some highlights of Wayne's musical life.

St Hilda's is an architectural award winning church on Wellington South Coast.
Take the Number One Bus to Island Bay and stop right outside

description:
Thundercat tickets, Presented by: Bench Music
Known for his jazz-fusion sensibility and elastic funk, Thundercat is bringing his 2026 tour to Wellington this May, performing live in support of his new album Distracted. See his show at Meow NUI in Wellington on Wed, 6 May 2026.
In an age of endless notifications and fractured attention, Thundercat turns the chaos inward on Distracted, his long-awaited new album and one of his most resonant statements yet. Six years on from It Is What It Is, the record captures the restless aftermath of grief and modern overload, balancing existential weight with Thundercat’s unmistakable wit and warmth. It’s an album that laughs through the noise, folding jazz-fusion virtuosity, elastic funk and late-night introspection into songs that feel like unfiltered inner monologues: tender, funny, and painfully human.
That philosophy carries directly into the live experience. Onstage, Thundercat treats Distracted not as a lecture on modern life, but as a shared release, stretching songs, breaking the fourth wall, leaning into humour and spontaneity as a form of survival.
Thundercat's New Zealand shows promise moments of presence inside the noise: music that drifts, refocuses, and reminds you why joy still matters. It’s playful, loose, deeply human, and a rare chance to experience Distracted and all of Thundercat the way it was meant to be felt, not scrolled past.
Thundercat - 'I Did This To Myself (feat. Lil Yachty)' (Official Audio)
Thundercat - 'I Wish I Didn't Waste Your Time'
Thundercat - 'No Moe Lies (feat. Tame Impala)' (Official Visualizer)

description:

Us Two is a high-energy house DJ act from the Midlands, UK, with releases on some of the world's most respected labels including Knee Deep In Sound, MicroHertz, and Eastenderz. Known for deep grooves, rolling basslines, and infectious crowd connection, Us Two has earned support from global heavyweights like Marco Carola, Jamie Jones, and Skream.
From European festival stages to Ibiza's 528 for a standout set with Knee Deep In Sound, Us Two's sound blends underground credibility with dancefloor-ready energy.
His latest release, "Simulation" (featuring Franksy), drops on Pete Tong's TSZR label, and has already earned plays from tastemakers like ANOTR, SG Lewis, LF SYSTEM, and SOSA, with early reactions going wild at Music On (Pacha Ibiza) and beyond.
For Lacuna, hosting Us Two’s debut in Wellington is about more than a headline — it's personal. We're thrilled to finally bring a dear friend's sound to our city and invite you to feel the energy first-hand on Saturday, 20 September 2025, at Meow Nui.

description:
Suzanne Vega, Widely regarded as one of the foremost songwriters of her generation, Suzanne Vega emerged as a leading figure of the folk revival of the early 1980s. Four decades later, she is still writing and singing great songs. In her Wellington show, Suzanne will perform a career-spanning set. See her on stage at St James Theatre performing favourites like Tom’s Diner, Luka, Marlene On The Wall, and plenty more. There’ll also be new songs from her 2025 album, ‘Flying with Angels’. Joining her on stage will be longtime guitarist, Gerry Leonard, and cellist Stephanie Winters. 
Since the release of her self-titled, critically acclaimed 1985 debut album, Suzanne Vega has played to sold-out concerts across the world. She is a masterful storyteller who, according to one The New York Times critique, “observes the world with a clinically poetic eye.” Her song lyrics often focus on city life, ordinary people, and real-world subjects. They are notably succinct, understated, and immediately recognisable.
label: Music & Concerts

description:
Mark William Lewis, London-based musician and songwriter Mark William Lewis brings his world tour to Australia and New Zealand for the very first time.

The genre-defying artist blends dream pop, indie rock, jazz and folk music, creating poetically sharp vivid landscapes. The tour follows the release of his self-titled album, a textually rich soundscape pairing poetic lyricism with his unmistakable voice and songs that explore intimacy and alienation, connection and disconnection.

Already deemed one of the best releases of 2025 (Vulture), Mark William Lewis’ timeless self-titled album moves between nostalgic, deep-rooted poems and upbeat, indie rock grooves. Presented as A24 Music’s first independent artist release.

description:
Acoustic Routes December Social Club with Mary Livingston, Featuring guest Mary Livingston. Mary has been part of our local folk scene since she arrived in Wellington 50 years ago, with forays into classical guitar and various choirs. She has sung with many people...in the early days it was with Dave Nicholson & Mary Hubble. Later, she linked up with Sarah Duckworth & then Robin Brew, the Magpies (acapella 4 part), and Dr Livingston and the Presumptions (band with Don and Jill). Her current singing partner is Sue Chamberlain although Mary and Robin are dusting off their voci for an airing in early 2026! Mary loves to harmonise, and aims to not just come up with something that sounds nice, but that actually tries to enhance the sound of the singer with the tune, - a technique learned from her barbershop-Sweet Adeline sojourn. Today however we are going to hear Mary as a soloist. She will share a range of songs that she likes to sing on her own, and puts the harmony in with her guitar work. Some will be her own songs, and the rest is likely to focus on her take on some Celtic favourites using a DADGAD tuning on her guitar. Some of the songs will be familiar to you, but others less so. She is pretty excited about doing this, as it is rare for her to sing alone.
In the second half, we’ll go around the circle and you’re welcome to lead a song or tune if you wish.
label: Music & Concerts

description:
Menopause The Musical, Described as the ultimate ladies’ night out, Menopause the Musical has been delivering laughs across the globe for 10 years. Now the fun-filled musical about ‘the change’ returns to Wellington for one night.  
Set in a department store, four women meet by chance while shopping for a black lace bra. Though they couldn’t be more different, they quickly discover a shared bond over memory loss, hot flushes, night sweats, and mood swings. What follows is a hilarious, heartwarming musical told with love, laughter, and the greatest hits of the 60s, 70s, and 80s. The laughter-filled 90-minute production gets audiences out of their seats dancing and singing along to clever parodies of beloved pop classics. 
Winner of over 40 Tony Awards, Menopause the Musical has entertained more than 17 million fans worldwide. It’s been translated into nine languages and performed in more than 500 cities. The show’s enduring appeal lies in its honesty, hilarity and heart. It celebrates women everywhere and turns shared experiences into shared joy.

description:
Yoko-Zuna: A Live Tribute to KAYTRANADA, This October, Aotearoa electronic/progressive hip-hop powerhouse Yoko-Zuna will make their long anticipated return to Pōneke, presenting the music of KAYTRANADA in the city's magnificent Meow NUI.
Over the past decade Yoko-Zuna have firmly established themselves as one of the country’s most exciting live acts and a favourite on the festival circuit. Just back from a world tour with hip-hop multihyphenate CHAII, the four piece made up of Swap Gomez, J.Y Lee, Kenji Iwamitsu-Holdaway, & Frank Eliesa once again join forces to perform their live tribute to a genre-defying beat wizard.
Louis Kevin Celestin aka KAYTRANADA is a Haitian-Canadian record producer and DJ. He first made waves in 2010 with a steady stream of mixtapes, remixes, and original tracks released under the name Kaytradamus. By 2013, reintroducing himself as KAYTRANADA, he was turning heads across the global music scene. The momentum only grew—by 2014, he landed a deal with the influential label XL Recordings. In 2016, he dropped his debut studio album, 99.9%, which was met with widespread critical acclaim. 2019’s Bubba—a genre-blurring triumph earned him two Grammy Awards, including Best Dance/Electronic Album, solidified his place as one of the most innovative voices in contemporary music.
Tickets on-sale 12.00PM Friday 4th July from meownui.com

description:
GLM (1985) + Texture, Noyer & Whaea G b2b Ohimesama Breaks (CUBA DUPA FUNDRAISER #`1), CUBA DUPA FUNDRAISER #1
Poneke, we’ve got another belter for you…
Ghost Media Presents:GLM (1985/Flexout/Incurzion)
+ Support from Texture, Noyer, Whaea G & Ohimesama Breaks
Get ready to party with Bristol’s finest!
We’ve invited GLM over to lay down his signature flavour of UK bass in Aotearoa for the first time.
Since his first release in 2023, GLM has curated a sound that incorporates elements of dnb, garage, dubstep, grime, and jungle, releasing music on respected independent labels including Incurzion Audio, Helix, Kojiki, Sub:stance Recordings, and 1985 Music. GLM’s cutting edge sonics have received radio support on networks like BBC Radio 1, Kiss FM, and Rinse FM, and he has performed widely across club circuits in the UK. A true don on the tools, we’re excited to welcome him as he showcases his production sound at Valhalla.
On support, we have an all-star lineup including TEXTURE, joining us from Otautahi for the first time since 2021.
We’ve been looking for an excuse to bring Texture up for a while, and this felt like a perfect opportunity. A purveyor of a unique sound that elicits an emotional response, fusing elements of bass, breakbeat, jungle and techno, Texture is high quality and well-seasoned, having opened for some serious artists including Addison Groove, Machinedrum, Ben UFO and Alix Perez. We can’t wait to host her for the first time this February!
From the local sphere, we’ve pulled out the big guns.
On opening duties we have NOYER, a man we can always trust to get the floor rocking. One half of Garage Sale, Noyer has been a staple of the capital’s underground dance music scene for the whole of the 2020s.
To close out the night in style, we’ve got a blockbuster back to back of two of the capital’s finest jungle selectors - WHAEA G & OHIMESAMA BREAKS. If either of those names are familiar to you, you’ll have some idea of the levels that are about to be effected.
As is standard, we’ve got BANSHEE SOUNDSYSTEM on ear and chest rattling duties. Come on!
Finally, we just want to mention that this event is a fundraiser for our Valhalla stage at Cuba Dupa. So buy a ticket and help support us for the biggest free party of the year!

description:
Fat Freddy's Drop: Based on a True Story 21st Anniversary Tour | Fri 1 May | Michael Fowler Centre, Fat Freddy's Drop: Based on a True Story 21st Anniversary Tour | Fri 1 May | Michael Fowler Centre
Doing it for the love of music, legendary band Fat Freddy's Drop are returning to the stage in 2026 to perform their ground-breaking debut album Based On A True Story.

The 51 dates across Australia, Aotearoa, Europe & UK will be Freddy’s first shows following the sudden death of founding band member Chris ‘MU’ Faiumu. The band is united in its decision to continue with this long-planned tour.

Lead singer Dallas Tamaira says, ‘It’s important for us to honour the intentions we had with MU and playing this album live is part of that.’

Based On A True Story was independently released in 2005, an album of 10 fresh slices of future funk, with surprises, fakes & freaky sidesteps. In a historic run, the album became Aotearoa’s Longest-Charting Album with 111 weeks in the Top 40, won eight NZ Music Awards and took out Giles Peterson’s Worldwide Music
Awards in the UK.

‘This was the band’s first full length studio album and its success was the springboard for what has been an incredible journey together’, explains saxophonist Scott Towers. ‘We want to pay tribute to the music, to MU and the band he established. And, of course, to play to our fans around the world who have supported us through everything.’
label: Music & Concerts

description:
[OFFSITE] Jim White (Aus) & Marisa Anderson (US) with Motte + UMU, [OFFSITE] Jim White (Aus) & Marisa Anderson (US) with Motte + UMU
Offsite at Meow, 9 Edward Street, Wellington

Jim White & Marisa Anderson
The collaboration between renowned drummer Jim White (Dirty Three, PJ Harvey, Bonnie Prince Billy, Cat Power) and acclaimed US guitarist Marisa Anderson is a natural union of two of the most intuitive players and listeners working in music. White and Anderson are both in high demand as collaborators, in no small part because of their mastery, versatility and highly expressive playing. The duo have amassed an impressive body of work together (including their 2020 debut The Quickening and 2024’s Swallowtail), and each remain at the vanguard of their practices due to an insatiable curiosity and delight in exploration of new avenues of expression.

---

Marisa Anderson channels the history of the guitar and stretches the boundaries of tradition. Her playing is fluid, emotional, and masterful, featuring compositions and improvisations that re-imagine the landscape of American music. Her work applies elements of minimalism, electronic music, drone and 20th century classical music to compositions based on blues, jazz, gospel and country music.

---

Drummer Jim White first come to attention in the mid-1990s with the acclaimed Australian instrumental trio Dirty Three, and is now recognised internationally as one of the most creative and expressive drummers on the planet. His unique playing style and deft way with sound and rhythm has seen him become the go-to drummer for a huge number of alt-A-list vocalists with collaboration in mind. PJ Harvey has said there is ballet in White’s light, precise touch. And the other collaborators that he’s mesmerised, including the likes of Bonnie Prince Billy, Smog, Nick Cave, White Magic, Beth Orton, Marianne Faithfull, Kurt Vile, Cat Power and the iconic Cretan lyra player Psarantonis, all know that there’s more to White’s drumming than mere accompaniment. His intuitive beats and singular approach have created sparks in his collaborations. Whoever White is playing with, he is right there, present in the most inspirational sense.

---

Motte is the solo project of violinist and composer, Anita Clark. Riding the thin line between beauty and devastation, Motte weaves together a richly textured world with a violin, delay pedal, sampled sound and voice. Creating a world solely her own, the sound is modern classical, ambient, alt/experimental pop and improvised noise. Her musical gifts and skilful reach across genres makes her an indispensable collaborator with The Phoenix Foundation, Luke Buda, Marlon Williams, Don McGlashan, the Renderers, Nadia Reid, Lawrence Arabia, Delaney Davidson and plenty of others. As Motte, she dances around the shining candescence of her culture like a night insect, always seeking a better vantage point to the light.

---

UMU is a duo of Riki Gooch and Daniel Beban. Silver Apples meets Kuduro with Riki's deep grooves played on an 80s Roland electronic drum kit which are manipulated and dubbed out by Dan on two Tama Techstar drum synths.

$35 earlybird tickets via Meow / $20 unwaged

Special thanks to CNZ for supporting Pyramid Club's programme
